gold_final_answer: All positive multiples of p; that is, n = k p for some positive integer k.
topics_flat: Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Fermat / Euler / Wilson theorems
Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Polynomials mod p
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Factorization techniques
prompt:
Let $p$ be a prime. Determine all positive integers $n$ for which the following condition is satisfied for all the integers $x$:
Condition: If $x^n - 1$ is divisible by $p$, then it is also divisible by $p^2$.
response_text:
We seek all positive integers \(n\) such that

\[
p \mid (x^n-1)\quad \Longrightarrow\quad p^2\mid (x^n-1)
\]

for **every integer** \(x\).

We will determine all such \(n\) in terms of the prime \(p\).

---

## Step 1: Reformulate modulo \(p\)

The condition says:

> For every integer \(x\), if \(x^n \equiv 1 \pmod p\), then actually \(x^n \equiv 1 \pmod{p^2}\).

If \(p\mid x\), then \(x^n\equiv 0\pmod p\), so such \(x\) never satisfy \(x^n\equiv1\pmod p\). Thus we only care about \(x\) coprime to \(p\).

So the question is about units modulo \(p\) and modulo \(p^2\).

---

## Step 2: Necessary condition from modulo \(p\)

The multiplicative group \((\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times\) has order \(p-1\), and is cyclic.

If there exists some \(a\not\equiv 1\pmod p\) with \(a^n\equiv 1\pmod p\), then our condition would force \(a^n\equiv1\pmod{p^2}\) as well.

In particular, every solution modulo \(p\) of \(X^n\equiv1\) must lift in this strong way.

Now, if \(\gcd(n,p-1)=d\), then modulo \(p\), the congruence \(x^n\equiv1\pmod p\) has exactly \(d\) solutions.

We will show that unless \(d=1\), the condition fails.

Indeed, let \(g\) be a primitive root modulo \(p^2\); then \(g\) mod \(p\) generates \((\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times\).  
Take

\[
x \equiv g^{(p-1)/d}\pmod{p^2}.
\]

Then modulo \(p\),

\[
x^n \equiv 1 \pmod p
\]

because its image has order dividing \(d=\gcd(n,p-1)\). But modulo \(p^2\), \(x\) has order divisible by \(p\) unless \(d=1\), so generally \(x^n\not\equiv1\pmod{p^2}\).

A cleaner way: since \((\mathbb Z/p^2\mathbb Z)^\times\) is cyclic of order \(p(p-1)\) for odd \(p\), the set of \(n\)-th roots of unity modulo \(p^2\) has size \(\gcd(n,p(p-1))\), while modulo \(p\) it has size \(\gcd(n,p-1)\). For every root mod \(p\) to lift to a root mod \(p^2\), these numbers must be equal:

\[
\gcd(n,p(p-1))=\gcd(n,p-1).
\]

This is equivalent to \(p\nmid n\).

But that alone is not enough: we also need every root mod \(p\) to lift uniquely, which in fact requires that modulo \(p\) there be only the trivial root \(1\). That means

\[
\gcd(n,p-1)=1.
\]

Together these imply \(\gcd(n,p(p-1))=1\), i.e.

\[
\gcd(n,p(p-1))=1.
\]

Since \(p\) is coprime to \(p-1\), this is equivalent to

\[
p\nmid n \quad\text{and}\quad \gcd(n,p-1)=1.
\]

But for odd \(p\), this is just \(\gcd(n,p(p-1))=1\).

---

## Step 3: Sufficiency

Assume

\[
\gcd(n,p(p-1))=1.
\]

Take any integer \(x\) such that \(p\mid x^n-1\), i.e.

\[
x^n\equiv1\pmod p.
\]

Then \(p\nmid x\), so \(x\) is a unit modulo \(p^2\). For odd \(p\), \((\mathbb Z/p^2\mathbb Z)^\times\) is cyclic of order \(p(p-1)\). Since \(n\) is coprime to this order, the map

\[
u\mapsto u^n
\]

is an automorphism of \((\mathbb Z/p^2\mathbb Z)^\times\). Hence the only solution of \(u^n\equiv1\pmod{p^2}\) is \(u\equiv1\pmod{p^2}\).

But from \(x^n\equiv1\pmod p\) and \(\gcd(n,p-1)=1\), already modulo \(p\) we get \(x\equiv1\pmod p\). Write \(x=1+kp\). Then

\[
x^n=(1+kp)^n \equiv 1+nkp \pmod{p^2}.
\]

If \(x^n\equiv1\pmod p\), this is automatic, but for divisibility by \(p^2\) we need \(p\mid nk\). Since \(p\nmid n\), this forces \(p\mid k\), hence \(x\equiv1\pmod{p^2}\), so indeed \(p^2\mid x^n-1\).

So the condition holds.

---

## Step 4: Special case \(p=2\)

If \(p=2\), the condition is:

\[
2\mid x^n-1 \implies 4\mid x^n-1.
\]

Now \(2\mid x^n-1\) means \(x\) is odd. For any odd \(x\),

- if \(n\) is even, then \(x^n\equiv1\pmod8\), certainly \(4\mid x^n-1\);
- if \(n\) is odd, take \(x=3\): then \(3^n\equiv3\pmod4\), so \(4\nmid 3^n-1\).

Thus for \(p=2\), exactly the even \(n\) work.

---

## Conclusion

- For \(p=2\): all **even** \(n\).
- For odd \(p\): exactly those \(n\) with
  \[
  \gcd(n,p(p-1))=1,
  \]
  equivalently
  \[
  p\nmid n \quad\text{and}\quad \gcd(n,p-1)=1.
  \]

Final answer: \(\boxed{\text{If }p=2,\text{ then all even }n;\ \text{if }p\text{ is odd, then exactly those }n\text{ such that } \gcd(n,p(p-1))=1.}\)
